history.text: Kingston was founded on 22 July 1692, shortly after the 1692 earthquake that devastated Port Royal; the original section of the city which was situated at the bottom of the Liguanea Plains was laid.
Thomas, on King Street, the chief thoroughfare, was first built before 1699 but was rebuilt after the earthquake in 1907.

history.label: 18th century
history.text: By the end of the 18th century, the city contained more than 3,000 brick buildings.
The town did not begin to grow until after the further destruction of Port Royal by fire in 1703.
By 1716, it had become the largest town and the centre of trade for Jamaica.
The first free school, Wolmers's, was founded in 1729 and there was a theatre, first on Harbour Street and then moved in 1774 to North Parade.
In 1755 the governor, Sir Charles Knowles, had decided to transfer the government offices from Spanish Town to Kingston.

However, by 1780 the population of Kingston was 11,000, and the merchants began lobbying for the administrative capital to be transferred from Spanish Town, which was by then eclipsed by the commercial activity in.
In 1907, 800 people died in another earthquake known as the 1907 Kingston earthquake, destroying nearly all the historical buildings south of Parade in the city.

history.label: 1990s
history.text: In the 1990s, crime increased in the region and several riots were reported, including one in 1999 against a rise of fuel prices.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: The earthquake survivors set up a camp on the sea front.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The government sold land to people with the regulation that they purchase no more than the amount of the land that they owned in Port Royal, and only land on the sea front.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Kingston took over the functions of Spanish Town (the capital at the time).

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The city became home to the Mona campus of the University of the West Indies.
